questions.q: A no-confidence motion is moved in which House of Parliament?
questions.explanation: A no-confidence motion is moved in the Lok Sabha, the House to which the government is responsible.

questions.q: What does a no-confidence motion test?
options: Whether the Council of Ministers still commands the confidence of the House
questions.explanation: It tests whether the Council of Ministers still has the confidence of the Lok Sabha.

questions.q: If a no-confidence motion is passed, what must the government do?
questions.explanation: If the motion is passed, the Council of Ministers is required to resign.

questions.q: Against whom is a no-confidence motion directed?
questions.explanation: A no-confidence motion is directed against the entire Council of Ministers collectively.

questions.q: Who can move a no-confidence motion?
options: A member of the Lok Sabha
questions.explanation: Any member of the Lok Sabha may move a no-confidence motion, subject to the rules.

questions.q: The principle that the Council of Ministers is answerable to the Lok Sabha is called what?
questions.explanation: The Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha, the basis of a no-confidence motion.

questions.q: Who presides over the Lok Sabha when a no-confidence motion is taken up?
questions.explanation: The Speaker of the Lok Sabha presides over its proceedings, including a no-confidence motion.

questions.q: A no-confidence motion needs to be decided by what?
options: An order of the Supreme Court
A decision of the President alone
questions.explanation: A no-confidence motion is decided by a vote of the members present and voting in the Lok Sabha.

questions.q: A no-confidence motion is an instrument of which branch holding the executive accountable?
questions.explanation: It is a legislative instrument through which Parliament holds the executive accountable.

questions.q: A no-confidence motion, if successful, can lead to the fall of which body?
questions.explanation: A successful no-confidence motion can bring down the Union government.

questions.q: A no-confidence motion in the Lok Sabha requires the support of how many members to be admitted?
options: A simple majority of the House
questions.explanation: Under the rules, the leave to move a no-confidence motion requires the support of at least 50 members.

questions.q: Under which rule of the Lok Sabha is a motion of no-confidence governed?
questions.explanation: Rule 198 of the Rules of Procedure of the Lok Sabha governs no-confidence motions.

questions.q: How does a no-confidence motion differ from a confidence motion?
options: A no-confidence motion is moved by the opposition; a confidence motion is sought by the government
They are identical in every way
A no-confidence motion is moved only in the Rajya Sabha
A confidence motion is decided by the courts
questions.explanation: A no-confidence motion is moved against the government, while a confidence motion is moved by the government to prove its majority.

questions.q: Is a no-confidence motion required to state any reasons or grounds when it is moved?
options: No, it need not set out grounds
Yes, it must cite specific laws
Yes, it must be approved by the President first
questions.explanation: A no-confidence motion need not state grounds; it simply expresses want of confidence in the Council of Ministers.

questions.q: A no-confidence motion is closely tied to which constitutional provision on collective responsibility?
questions.explanation: Article 75 states that the Council of Ministers is collectively responsible to the Lok Sabha, the constitutional basis for the motion.

questions.q: A motion expressing disapproval of a specific government policy, distinct from a no-confidence motion, is known as what?
questions.explanation: A censure motion criticises specific policies or actions, unlike a no-confidence motion against the whole government.

questions.q: Once leave to move a no-confidence motion is granted, the Speaker generally does what?
options: Allots a day or days for its discussion
Refers it to the Supreme Court
Sends it to the Rajya Sabha
Forwards it to the President for assent
questions.explanation: After leave is granted, the Speaker fixes a day or days for discussion of the motion.

questions.q: A no-confidence motion can be moved against the government only when the Lok Sabha is in what state?
questions.explanation: A no-confidence motion can be moved only while the Lok Sabha is in session.

questions.q: If a no-confidence motion is defeated, what is the immediate effect on the government?
questions.explanation: If the motion is defeated, the government retains the confidence of the House and continues in office.

questions.q: A no-confidence motion is decided by which kind of majority in the Lok Sabha?
options: A simple majority of members present and voting
questions.explanation: A no-confidence motion is carried by a simple majority of members present and voting.

questions.q: At the state level, a no-confidence motion against a state government is moved in which body?
questions.explanation: At the state level, a no-confidence motion is moved in the Legislative Assembly, to which the state ministry is responsible.

questions.q: A no-confidence motion cannot be moved in which House because the Council of Ministers is not responsible to it?
options: The Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha both
questions.explanation: The Council of Ministers is not responsible to the Rajya Sabha, so a no-confidence motion cannot be moved there.

questions.q: The first no-confidence motion against a government in independent India was moved in 1963 against the ministry led by whom?
questions.explanation: The first no-confidence motion in the Lok Sabha, in 1963, was moved against the government headed by Jawaharlal Nehru.

questions.q: Who moved the first no-confidence motion in the Lok Sabha in 1963?
questions.explanation: Acharya J.B. Kripalani moved the first no-confidence motion in the Lok Sabha in 1963.

questions.q: In 1979, the government of which Prime Minister resigned in connection with the loss of majority during a no-confidence situation?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister Morarji Desai resigned in 1979 amid a no-confidence motion, before the vote was completed.

questions.q: In 1999, the government led by Atal Bihari Vajpayee lost a confidence vote in the Lok Sabha by what margin?
questions.explanation: In 1999, the Vajpayee-led government lost a confidence motion in the Lok Sabha by a single vote.

questions.q: Distinguish a no-confidence motion from an adjournment motion: the latter is primarily used to do what?
options: Draw attention to a recent matter of urgent public importance
questions.explanation: An adjournment motion is used to discuss a definite matter of urgent public importance, unlike a no-confidence motion against the government itself.

questions.q: Unlike a no-confidence motion, the rejection of which type of motion in the Lok Sabha is also treated as a loss of confidence in the government?
options: A confidence motion sought by the government
questions.explanation: Defeat of a confidence motion that the government itself moves is also treated as the government losing the House's confidence.

questions.q: A no-confidence motion differs from the budget's defeat, but the failure of the government to pass which kind of bill is also regarded as loss of confidence?
options: A money bill or demand for grants
A bill referred to a select committee
questions.explanation: Defeat of a money bill or the demands for grants is conventionally treated as a loss of confidence in the government.

questions.q: If a no-confidence motion succeeds and no alternative government can be formed, the President may, on advice, do what regarding the Lok Sabha?
options: Convert it into the Rajya Sabha
Dissolve it and order fresh elections
Transfer its powers to the judiciary
questions.explanation: Where no alternative government is possible, the Lok Sabha may be dissolved and fresh elections held.
